cards.front: The basic unit of a chemical element, consisting of a nucleus surrounded by electrons.
A group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds; the smallest particle of a compound.
An atom or molecule that has gained or lost one or more electrons, giving it a net electrical charge.
The three main particles that make up an atom.
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom; it uniquely identifies an element.
The total number of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
Atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons.
A positively charged ion (formed when an atom loses electrons).
A negatively charged ion (formed when an atom gains electrons).
The state of matter with a definite shape and volume.
The state of matter with a definite volume, but no definite shape; it takes the shape of its container.
The state of matter with no definite shape or volume; it expands to fill its container.
A change in the form or state of matter, but not its chemical identity (e.g., melting ice, boiling water).
A process that changes the chemical identity of a substance, forming new substances (e.g., rusting iron, burning wood).
The technique used to separate an insoluble solid from a liquid by passing it through a porous material.
The technique used to separate a dissolved solid from a liquid by evaporating the liquid.
The technique used to separate two liquids with different boiling points.
The technique used to separate mixtures based on the different speeds at which components travel through a medium.
A pure substance consisting entirely of one type of atom, which cannot be broken down by chemical means.
A substance made of two or more different elements that are chemically bonded together in a fixed ratio.
A combination of two or more pure substances that are physically combined and can be separated by physical means.
A mixture where the components are not uniformly distributed (e.g., salad, sand).
A mixture with a uniform composition throughout; also called a solution (e.g., salt water, air).
The principle that matter is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
A chemical bond formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons.
A chemical bond formed by the complete transfer of electrons from one atom to another, resulting in oppositely charged ions.
The most important piece of safety equipment that must be worn at all times in the lab.
The first action to take if a chemical splashes into your eyes.
cards.back: Use the eyewash station for 15-20 minutes

cards.front: The type of footwear required in a chemistry lab.
The safe technique for smelling a chemical in the lab.
The final, essential step to complete before leaving the laboratory.
